skill.name: github-project-management
skill.description: Manage GitHub issues, projects, boards, sprints, and swarm-coordinated workflows using GitHub CLI and automation examples.
skill.summary: Coordinate GitHub issue triage, project boards, sprint planning, and swarm task workflows.
skill.author: Claude Code

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and high-risk keyword patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no prompt injection, malware intent, or real weak cryptography, but confirmed extensive GitHub CLI, npx, and MCP examples that can mutate repositories and project boards. Publish with a clear warning that users must review commands and permissions before execution.

medium_findings.title: External Package and Shell Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many Bash examples using gh, jq, command substitution, and npx packages such as claude-flow and ruv-swarm. This is relevant to GitHub automation, but it executes local commands and third-party package code, so users should approve each command and verify package trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show shell commands and npx execution. Confidence is not higher because these are documented operational examples for the stated GitHub management purpose, not hidden execution code.

medium_findings.title: Repository and Project State Mutation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create, edit, comment on, label, close, and add GitHub issues and project items. These actions are legitimate for project management, but mistakes or overbroad permissions could alter production repositories or close active issues.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly perform GitHub write operations. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the behavior matches the skill description.

low_findings.title: External Webhook Endpoint Example
low_findings.description: The board realtime example includes a placeholder HTTPS webhook endpoint. It does not show credential exfiltration, but users should replace it with a trusted endpoint and avoid sending private repository data to unapproved services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoint is explicitly present and tied to board synchronization. It appears to be a placeholder domain, so the finding is low severity.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Keyword Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings did not correspond to cryptographic operations during review. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom encryption, credential handling, or obfuscated security logic in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found prose and shell examples rather than cryptographic code at representative flagged locations. The static labels are therefore false positives.

low_findings.title: Device File Redirection Dismissed
low_findings.description: The /dev/null usage suppresses stderr for a GitHub label removal command. This is a common shell pattern and not sensitive filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line shows stderr redirection only. There is no file read, write, deletion, or traversal behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Execution of Third-Party Automation Packages
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands execute claude-flow and ruv-swarm through npx. This can run package code from the configured package registry and should be pinned or reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines show direct npx-based execution. The commands are not hidden, but they are still a meaningful execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated GitHub Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples automate issue creation, edits, comments, labels, project item additions, and stale issue handling. These operations require scoped permissions and human review in sensitive repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands visibly mutate GitHub resources. The risk is confirmed, while intent remains aligned with the project management use case.

content.user_title: Coordinate GitHub Projects with AI Workflows
content.value_statement: GitHub project data often spreads across issues, boards, milestones, and team updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for coordinated issue triage, board sync, sprint planning, and progress reporting.
actual_capabilities: Creates structured GitHub issue workflows with labels, assignees, comments, and swarm coordination steps.
Shows how to convert issues into decomposed tasks and track progress through automated comments.
Provides GitHub Projects board setup, field mapping, card synchronization, and bulk board operations.
Supports sprint, milestone, kanban, analytics, KPI, and release planning workflows.
Includes templates for bugs, features, integration tasks, and swarm-ready GitHub issues.
Documents troubleshooting commands for board sync, performance optimization, and data recovery.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and appropriate repository or organization permissions.
Many examples depend on ruv-swarm or claude-flow commands that must be installed and trusted.
The skill provides workflow patterns, not a complete standalone project management application.
Automated issue edits, closures, and board updates can be disruptive without human review.

use_cases.title: Triage Incoming Issues
use_cases.description: Classify, label, assign, and decompose new GitHub issues into clear implementation tasks.

use_cases.title: Maintain Project Boards
use_cases.description: Synchronize issue status with GitHub Projects fields, columns, views, and board metadata.

use_cases.title: Plan Sprints and Releases
use_cases.description: Build sprint plans, track milestone progress, report blockers, and estimate delivery risks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Project Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a single issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the GitHub project management skill to draft a well-structured GitHub issue for this feature: [feature]. Include labels, acceptance criteria, and a short implementation plan. Do not run commands until I approve them.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Open Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the open issues in this repository and propose labels, priorities, owners, and duplicate candidates. Show the planned GitHub CLI commands first and wait for confirmation.

prompt_templates.title: Synchronize a Project Board
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a board synchronization workflow for project [project name]. Map issue labels and statuses to board fields, identify required permissions, and provide safe commands for review.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Release Sprint
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow across issues, milestones, and reports.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a sprint and release plan for milestone [milestone]. Include issue dependencies, capacity risks, board updates, progress reporting, and commands that I can approve before execution.

output_examples.input: Plan issue triage for a repository with unlabeled bugs and feature requests.
output_examples.output: A triage plan with recommended labels, priority rules, duplicate checks, owner suggestions, and a review queue before any GitHub edits.

output_examples.input: Set up a GitHub Projects board for a two-week sprint.
output_examples.output: A board design with status columns, sprint fields, WIP limits, milestone tracking, reporting cadence, and approval-gated setup commands.

output_examples.input: Report project health for the current release milestone.
output_examples.output: A summary of completed work, blocked issues, cycle-time risks, dependency concerns, and recommended next actions for the team.

best_practices: Run commands in read-only or dry-run mode first when available.
Use least-privilege GitHub tokens and confirm the target owner, repository, and project before writes.
Review automated comments, labels, and closures before applying them to active repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not run npx automation packages without verifying the package source and version.
Do not enable bidirectional board sync until field mappings and conflict rules are reviewed.
Do not auto-close stale issues in customer-facing repositories without a team-approved policy.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. Most command examples use gh and require an authenticated GitHub CLI session.

faq.question: Can it modify my repository?
faq.answer: Yes. Several workflows create, edit, label, comment on, and close GitHub issues or project items.

faq.question: Does it support GitHub Projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes board setup, item import, field mapping, synchronization, analytics, and dashboard examples.

faq.question: Is swarm tooling required?
faq.answer: Some workflows depend on ruv-swarm or claude-flow. Basic GitHub issue planning can still be adapted without them.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Start with planning output, review proposed commands, and use limited GitHub permissions for execution.

faq.question: Can it manage private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, if the authenticated account has access. Private data should not be sent to unapproved webhooks or services.
